text: The surgeon lifts a large fibroid, measuring approximately eight to nine centimeters, from behind the uterus, revealing its size and position relative to the uterine structure.
The surgeon injects a chemical at the junction between the fibroid and the uterus, causing the uterus to turn white as the blood supply is reduced.
The surgeon cuts through the outer capsule of the fibroid, revealing the fibroid itself as it emerges from the thick surrounding capsule.
The surgeon inserts a myoma screw into the fibroid with the right hand, using the tool to pull and peel the fibroid away from the surrounding tissue, resembling the peeling of an orange.
The surgeon applies traction with one hand while peeling off the outer capsule with the other hand, progressively exposing more of the fibroid as it emerges from the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon uses an electric current to cut the large fibroid from the right side of the uterus, visibly separating the fibroid from the surrounding uterine tissue.
The surgeon removes the last few fibers of the fibroid, completing the excision as the fibroid is fully detached from the uterine tissue.
The surgeon identifies a small fibroid located behind the uterus and proceeds to remove it, visually detaching the fibroid from the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon observes the large posterior flap of the capsule, revealing a significant gap that requires closure.
The surgeon trims the posterior flap of the capsule to reduce its size, ensuring it fits well with the anterior flap and eliminates any excess tissue.
The surgeon stitches the outer layer of the capsule in two layers, ensuring there are no dead spaces or areas for blood collection between the capsule and the uterus.
The surgeon performs intricate stitching inside the uterus using laparoscopic techniques, carefully closing the surgical site after fibroid removal.

The surgeon cleans the inside of the uterus and identifies a small fibroid, approximately two to three centimeters in size, located on top of the uterus. The fibroid is removed using the same technique, involving cutting the outer capsule.

The surgeon pulls and peels the fibroid away from the surrounding capsule. The bed is then sutured to oppose the margins, ensuring no dead spaces are left. The sutured area appears to be healing well.
The surgeon uses a morcellator to shred the large fibroid inside the abdominal cavity into small pieces, facilitating its removal through the small incisions on the surface.

The morcellator blade rotates around the fibroid capsule, cutting it into long threads. These fibroid pieces are carefully extracted through a small incision, ensuring the sharp blade does not contact surrounding tissues.

The surgeon continues to cut chunks from the fibroid, visibly reducing its size. With the fibroid tissue removed, the uterus is restored to its normal shape, and the large fibroids have been successfully excised.

The surgeon meticulously cleans the uterus, peritoneal cavity, ovaries, fallopian tubes, and abdominal area, removing all visible blood, debris, and small tissue fragments to ensure a clean pelvic region at the conclusion of the myomectomy procedure.
